🍔 FoodHub Order Data – Exploratory Data Analysis

Full-Code Submission  |  Data Science Learner Notebook  |  Dataset: foodhub_order.csv

0. Import Libraries & Load Data

In [ ]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

pd.set_option('display.max_columns', None)
pd.set_option('display.float_format', '{:.2f}'.format)
sns.set_style("whitegrid")
plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 120

1. Data Understanding

Shape, data types, missing values & basic statistics

📋 Key Observations

  • The dataset contains 1,898 orders with 9 features — no missing values.
  • rating uses "Not given" as a placeholder — requires conversion before numeric analysis.
  • Average order cost is $16.50; average total delivery wait is 51.5 minutes.
  • Food preparation time ranges from 20–35 min; delivery time from 15–33 min.
In [ ]:
# Feature engineering
df['rating_num'] = df['rating'].apply(lambda x: np.nan if x == 'Not given' else int(x))
df['total_time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']
print(f"Ratings provided : {df['rating_num'].notna().sum()} / {len(df)}")
print(f"Unrated orders   : {(df['rating']=='Not given').sum()} ({(df['rating']=='Not given').mean()*100:.1f}%)")
Ratings provided : 1162 / 1898 Unrated orders : 736 (38.8%)

2. Order Cost Analysis

Distribution, spread and segmentation of order values

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
sns.histplot(df['cost_of_the_order'], bins=30, color='#F4A261', edgecolor='white', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].axvline(df['cost_of_the_order'].mean(), color='#E76F51', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f"Mean ${df['cost_of_the_order'].mean():.2f}")
axes[0].set_title('Distribution of Order Cost'); axes[0].legend(); axes[0].set_xlabel('Cost ($)')
sns.boxplot(y=df['cost_of_the_order'], color='#E76F51', ax=axes[1], width=0.4)
axes[1].set_title('Order Cost - Box Plot'); axes[1].set_ylabel('Cost ($)')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: The cost distribution is right-skewed. Most orders cluster between $10–$25, with a mean of $16.50. High-value orders (>$20) represent 29.2% of all orders and are key revenue drivers. A handful of orders near $35 represent premium spending.

3. Preparation & Delivery Time Analysis

Understanding time components and total delivery experience

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
sns.histplot(df['food_preparation_time'], bins=20, color='#2A9D8F', edgecolor='white', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].axvline(df['food_preparation_time'].mean(), color='#E76F51', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f"Mean {df['food_preparation_time'].mean():.1f} min")
axes[0].set_title('Food Preparation Time'); axes[0].legend(); axes[0].set_xlabel('Minutes')

sns.histplot(df['delivery_time'], bins=20, color='#264653', edgecolor='white', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].axvline(df['delivery_time'].mean(), color='#E76F51', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f"Mean {df['delivery_time'].mean():.1f} min")
axes[1].set_title('Delivery Time'); axes[1].legend(); axes[1].set_xlabel('Minutes')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: Food preparation averages 27.4 min and delivery 24.2 min, totalling 51.5 min average wait. Preparation accounts for ~53% of total wait time. Both distributions show a nearly uniform/multimodal spread suggesting restaurant batching patterns.
🎯 Recommendation: Since food prep dominates wait time, FoodHub should work with restaurants on kitchen efficiency — especially during peak weekend hours.

4. Customer Rating Analysis

Rating distribution and engagement rate

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
rating_counts = df['rating'].value_counts()
axes[0].bar(rating_counts.index, rating_counts.values,
            color=['#F4A261','#E76F51','#2A9D8F','#264653'], edgecolor='white')
axes[0].set_title('All Rating Values'); axes[0].set_xlabel('Rating'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Count')

df_rated = df[df['rating'] != 'Not given'].copy()
df_rated['rating'] = df_rated['rating'].astype(int)
rated_counts = df_rated['rating'].value_counts().sort_index()
axes[1].pie(rated_counts, labels=[str(r) for r in rated_counts.index],
            autopct='%1.1f%%', colors=['#E76F51','#2A9D8F','#264653'],
            startangle=90, wedgeprops={'edgecolor':'white','linewidth':2})
axes[1].set_title('Among Rated Orders')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: 38.8% of customers never rated their order. Among those who did, 50.6% awarded a perfect 5, with 33.2% rating 4. The average among rated orders is 4.34/5 — indicating high satisfaction, but a large silent majority.
🎯 Recommendation: Introduce post-delivery push notifications and in-app incentives (loyalty points) to close the rating gap. A 10% improvement would yield ~190 additional data points for performance monitoring.

5. Cuisine Type Analysis

Demand and revenue breakdown by cuisine

In [ ]:
cuisine_counts  = df['cuisine_type'].value_counts()
cuisine_revenue = df.groupby('cuisine_type')['cost_of_the_order'].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))
axes[0].barh(cuisine_counts.index, cuisine_counts.values,
             color=sns.color_palette('tab10', len(cuisine_counts)), edgecolor='white')
axes[0].invert_yaxis(); axes[0].set_title('Orders by Cuisine Type'); axes[0].set_xlabel('Number of Orders')

axes[1].barh(cuisine_revenue.index, cuisine_revenue.values,
             color=sns.color_palette('tab10', len(cuisine_revenue)), edgecolor='white')
axes[1].invert_yaxis(); axes[1].set_title('Revenue by Cuisine Type'); axes[1].set_xlabel('Total Revenue ($)')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: American cuisine leads with 584 orders (~31%), followed by Japanese (470, ~25%) and Italian (298, ~16%). These three cuisines account for ~72% of all orders and revenue. Niche cuisines like Korean, Vietnamese, and Spanish have very low representation (<15 orders each).
🎯 Recommendation: Invest in marketing for American, Japanese, and Italian cuisine restaurants. Launch targeted campaigns for mid-tier cuisines (Mexican, Indian, Chinese) to diversify and grow market share.

6. Top Restaurants by Demand

In [ ]:
top10 = df['restaurant_name'].value_counts().head(10)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
colors = sns.color_palette('tab10', 10)
bars = ax.barh(top10.index, top10.values, color=colors, edgecolor='white')
for b in bars:
    ax.text(b.get_width()+1, b.get_y()+b.get_height()/2, str(int(b.get_width())),
            va='center', fontweight='bold')
ax.invert_yaxis()
ax.set_title('Top 10 Restaurants by Number of Orders', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')
ax.set_xlabel('Number of Orders')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: Shake Shack dominates with 219 orders — 1.7× more than the 2nd-ranked The Meatball Shop (132). The top 5 restaurants account for over 33% of all orders. This concentration signals strong brand loyalty but also a platform dependency risk.
🎯 Recommendation: Negotiate premium partnerships with top-performing restaurants. Simultaneously, recruit and promote mid-tier restaurants to reduce concentration risk and provide customers more variety.

7. Weekday vs Weekend Analysis

Order volume, cost and time patterns by day type

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))
day_counts = df['day_of_the_week'].value_counts()
axes[0].pie(day_counts, labels=day_counts.index, autopct='%1.1f%%',
            colors=['#F4A261','#2A9D8F'], startangle=90,
            wedgeprops={'edgecolor':'white','linewidth':2})
axes[0].set_title('Orders: Weekday vs Weekend')

day_cost = df.groupby('day_of_the_week')['cost_of_the_order'].mean()
axes[1].bar(day_cost.index, day_cost.values, color=['#F4A261','#2A9D8F'], width=0.5, edgecolor='white')
axes[1].set_title('Avg Order Cost'); axes[1].set_ylabel('Cost ($)')
for i, v in enumerate(day_cost.values): axes[1].text(i, v+0.1, f'${v:.2f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

day_total = df.groupby('day_of_the_week')['total_time'].mean()
axes[2].bar(day_total.index, day_total.values, color=['#F4A261','#2A9D8F'], width=0.5, edgecolor='white')
axes[2].set_title('Avg Total Delivery Time'); axes[2].set_ylabel('Minutes')
for i, v in enumerate(day_total.values): axes[2].text(i, v+0.1, f'{v:.1f}m', ha='center', fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: 71.2% of all orders are placed on weekends — more than 2.5× weekday volume. Weekend orders also carry a slightly higher average cost, suggesting leisure-driven, potentially larger group orders.
🎯 Recommendation: Ensure surge capacity in delivery staff on weekends. Use weekday slow periods for restaurant onboarding and targeted lunch-break promotions for busy professionals.

8. Cost vs Rating & Cuisine

Does higher spending correlate with better ratings?

In [ ]:
df_rated = df[df['rating'] != 'Not given'].copy()
df_rated['rating'] = df_rated['rating'].astype(int)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
rating_cost = df_rated.groupby('rating')['cost_of_the_order'].mean()
axes[0].bar(rating_cost.index, rating_cost.values,
            color=['#E76F51','#2A9D8F','#264653'], width=0.5, edgecolor='white')
axes[0].set_title('Avg Cost by Rating'); axes[0].set_xlabel('Rating'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Avg Cost ($)')
for i, v in enumerate(rating_cost.values): axes[0].text(i, v+0.1, f'${v:.2f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')

cuisine_cost = df.groupby('cuisine_type')['cost_of_the_order'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
axes[1].barh(cuisine_cost.index, cuisine_cost.values,
             color=sns.color_palette('tab10', len(cuisine_cost)), edgecolor='white')
axes[1].invert_yaxis(); axes[1].set_title('Avg Cost by Cuisine'); axes[1].set_xlabel('Avg Cost ($)')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: Rating and cost are not strongly correlated — customers rating 3 and 5 spend similar amounts on average. Korean and Spanish cuisines have the highest avg cost (~$24–$26), while American (the most popular) averages ~$14. Premium price does not guarantee a better rating.

9. Correlation Analysis & Total Time Distribution

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
num_cols = df[['cost_of_the_order','food_preparation_time','delivery_time','total_time']]
sns.heatmap(num_cols.corr(), annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm',
            ax=axes[0], linewidths=0.5, square=True, cbar_kws={'shrink':0.8})
axes[0].set_title('Correlation Heatmap')

sns.histplot(df['total_time'], bins=25, color='#E9C46A', edgecolor='white', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].axvline(df['total_time'].mean(), color='#E76F51', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f"Mean {df['total_time'].mean():.1f} min")
axes[1].set_title('Total Time Distribution (Prep + Delivery)'); axes[1].set_xlabel('Minutes')
axes[1].legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: Numerical features show very low inter-correlation — cost, preparation time, and delivery time are largely independent variables. Total wait time averages 51.5 minutes, spanning ~35–68 minutes. The near-uniform spread indicates consistent but variable kitchen and delivery operations.

10. Rating by Cuisine & Day of Week

Which cuisines and time periods earn the best customer feedback?

In [ ]:
df_rated = df[df['rating'] != 'Not given'].copy()
df_rated['rating'] = df_rated['rating'].astype(int)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 4))
avg_rating_cuisine = df_rated.groupby('cuisine_type')['rating'].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
axes[0].barh(avg_rating_cuisine.index, avg_rating_cuisine.values,
             color=sns.color_palette('tab10', len(avg_rating_cuisine)), edgecolor='white')
axes[0].invert_yaxis(); axes[0].set_title('Avg Rating by Cuisine'); axes[0].set_xlabel('Avg Rating')
for i, v in enumerate(avg_rating_cuisine.values):
    axes[0].text(v+0.01, i, f'{v:.2f}', va='center', fontsize=9)

avg_rating_day = df_rated.groupby('day_of_the_week')['rating'].mean()
axes[1].bar(avg_rating_day.index, avg_rating_day.values, color=['#F4A261','#2A9D8F'], width=0.5, edgecolor='white')
axes[1].set_ylim(3.5, 5); axes[1].set_title('Avg Rating by Day Type'); axes[1].set_ylabel('Avg Rating')
for i, v in enumerate(avg_rating_day.values): axes[1].text(i, v+0.01, f'{v:.2f}', ha='center', fontweight='bold')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: Korean and Vietnamese cuisines score highest on average, though with very few orders. Among high-volume cuisines, Japanese and Italian maintain strong ratings (~4.4). Weekend ratings are marginally higher than weekday, possibly due to more relaxed expectations or premium restaurant selection.

11. Order Value Segmentation & Rating Gaps

In [ ]:
df['high_value'] = df['cost_of_the_order'] > 20
hv_day = df.groupby(['day_of_the_week','high_value']).size().unstack(fill_value=0)
hv_day.columns = ['<=\$20','> \$20']

unrated_pct = df.groupby('cuisine_type').apply(
    lambda x: (x['rating']=='Not given').sum()/len(x)*100
).sort_values(ascending=False)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))
hv_day.plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[0], color=['#E76F51','#2A9D8F'], edgecolor='white', rot=0)
axes[0].set_title('High-Value Orders (>$20) by Day Type')
axes[0].legend(title='Order Value'); axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of Orders')

axes[1].barh(unrated_pct.index, unrated_pct.values,
             color=sns.color_palette('tab10', len(unrated_pct)), edgecolor='white')
axes[1].invert_yaxis(); axes[1].set_title('% Unrated Orders by Cuisine')
axes[1].set_xlabel('% Not Rated')
overall_pct = (df['rating']=='Not given').mean()*100
axes[1].axvline(overall_pct, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2,
                label=f'Overall {overall_pct:.1f}%')
axes[1].legend()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
💡 Insight: High-value orders (>$20) are far more prevalent on weekends — consistent with group dining and leisure spending. Mediterranean and Southern cuisines show the highest unrated rates, well above the overall 38.8% average — indicating engagement gaps in those segments.

12. Summary & Business Recommendations

🔍 Key Findings

  • Volume: 1,898 orders; no missing values. Weekends drive 71.2% of demand.
  • Revenue: Average order is $16.50; 29.2% of orders exceed $20.
  • Time: Total wait averages 51.5 min; food prep (~27.4 min) is the bigger contributor.
  • Cuisine: American, Japanese, and Italian account for ~72% of orders.
  • Restaurants: Shake Shack alone handles 11.5% of orders — a concentration risk.
  • Ratings: 38.8% go unrated; rated orders average 4.34/5.

🎯 Business Recommendations

1. Boost Rating Collection: Use in-app prompts and loyalty rewards to cut the 38.8% unrated gap. Better data enables better restaurant performance tracking.
2. Optimize Food Prep Time: Prep accounts for ~53% of total wait. Partner with restaurants on kitchen throughput improvements, especially on weekends.
3. Weekend Surge Strategy: 71.2% of orders hit on weekends. Ensure adequate delivery staff and use dynamic incentives to manage peaks.
4. Diversify Restaurant Portfolio: Reduce reliance on top-5 restaurants by recruiting and promoting mid-tier options to build resilience.
5. Target High-Value Customers: The 29.2% of orders above $20 represent disproportionate revenue. Create loyalty tiers and premium bundles for this segment.
6. Grow Underpenetrated Cuisines: Indian, Mexican, and Middle Eastern cuisines are underserved given NYC's diversity. Targeted restaurant acquisition and marketing could unlock significant demand.
